titleStmt.title: Richard Glas
born Vienna, August 14, 1890; died London, December 12, 1973

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.head: Career Summary
div.p: Richard Glas was born in Vienna in 1890. Nothing is known of his earliest life. He was a member of the Piano Masterclass (Meisterschule) of the virtuoso performer Leopold Godowsky at the Vienna Conservatory, receiving the highest award in 1908: a Bösendorfer parlor grand piano together with the Society [of the Friends of Music]’s Silver Medal (Welt Blatt, July 7). He was spoken of at the time as Godowsky’s “best pupil,” and Godowsky wrote a recommendation for him: “I am extremely cautious when recommending an artist, but in the case of Glas I have no hesitation in doing so” (Musical Times April 1952, p. 183); around that time, he was appointed professor by the Imperial-Russian Society of Music of Tbilisi in Georgia. Glas married the Russian pianist Inna Alexandrowna Fedorowa on November 12, 1914. The couple were both Jewish. Thereafter Richard was imprisoned in Siberia for most of World War I.

After the war, Glas was appointed Head of the Piano Masterclass of the Kiel Conservatory, where he became acquainted with Reinhard Oppel, who had been teaching music theory there since 1911. Glas was a keen advocate for Oppel’s compositions, and included the latter’s piano works in his concert programs and lobbied others on Oppel’s behalf, Oppel reciprocating in 1920 by dedicating to Glas his second piano sonata, Op. 25. In 1925 Oppel wrote a four-hand sonata for Richard and Inna (OC 52/637), and in the same year an Etude in D-flat major for Inna.

Oppel and Glas both quit the Kiel Conservatory in 1924 in dissatisfaction at working conditions. Glas remained in Kiel making headway in his career as a concert pianist, while Oppel moved to Kiel University to teach from 1924 to 1931 and thereafter to Leipzig until his death in 1941. In 1932 Glas was appointed Director of the Schleswig-Holstein Conservatory of Music; by 1933, however, he was already encountering difficulties from the Nazi authorities and was forced to relinquish the directorship of the Conservatory. In 1935 he and his wife moved to Prague.

Glas maintained a Europe-wide concert career for many years, playing mainly Classical and Romantic repertory (Beethoven sonatas and concertos, Brahms concertos and piano pieces, Schubert, Chopin and Liszt pieces). In February 1913 he scored a major success in St. Petersburg playing with the Court Orchestra; he was by then also performing evening piano recitals (Klavierabende) the first recorded of which, in April 1913, took place in the Beethoven-Saal in Vienna and included Beethoven’s “Appassionata” Sonata and the Liszt “Mephisto” Waltzes. After his difficult war years, Glas had resumed his concert work by 1921, performing throughout the 1920s and first half of the 1930s. Presumably excluded from German concert halls from 1933, he continued to play in Austria in 1936 and 1937

After the annexation of Austria in March 1938, Richard and Inna emigrated to London. By 1939 their address was “Knip Hill,” Eaton Park Road, Cobham, Surrey. With the onset of World War II Glas was interned as a “male enemy alien” in June 1940, then released in December of that year (UK World War II Alien Internees). In 1948 Richard received his British naturalisation papers (National Archives HP 334/204.39027). He taught for a time at Wynstones School in Gloucester, and then at the London College of Music, Great Marlborough Street in central London, where by 1951 he was Head of the Piano Masterclass. At the same time he tried to rebuild his concert career; however, as one former pupil of his reported, he faced a lot of “racial prejudice.” He and Inna also gave private lessons and coaching at their home, 28a Landsdowne Road, Holland Park. Nothing more is known of his career or of the remainder of Inna’s life. At his death in 1973, Richard was living in Kensington, London.

div.head: Richard Glas and Schenker
div.p: Schenker learned of Glas first from Oppel’s correspondence in 1921, and thereafter received frequent reports. Glas visited Schenker in Vienna on two occasions, in June 1922 and in April 1929, with an attempt also in September 1924 that did not come to fruition. Schenker also wrote to Glas on one occasion in 1926.
